parts.content: Write the initiation step for the reaction between $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ and $\mathrm{CH}_4$.
parts.markscheme: $\mathrm{Cl}_2 \to 2\mathrm{Cl}\cdot$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the initiation step
steps.content: In the free-radical substitution mechanism between an alkane and a halogen, the reaction begins with the **initiation step**. This step involves the formation of reactive radicals from a stable molecule. This topic is covered in **Reactivity 3.3: Electron sharing reactions**.

highlights.text: Write the initiation step for the reaction between $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ and $\mathrm{CH}_4$

steps.title: Describe homolytic fission
steps.content: Initiation occurs when the bond in the chlorine molecule ($Cl_2$) breaks. Because the two atoms have the same electronegativity, the bond undergoes **homolytic fission**. This means each chlorine atom takes one electron from the shared pair, usually under the influence of UV light, resulting in two neutral chlorine radicals ($Cl\cdot$).

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
We represent this process as a balanced chemical equation. We start with one molecule of $Cl_2$ and end with two $Cl\cdot$ radicals. Note that the methane ($CH_4$) is not involved in this first step; it only reacts later during the propagation stage.

$$\mathrm{Cl}_2 \to 2\mathrm{Cl}\cdot$$

parts.content: State what happens to the electrons in this initiation step.
parts.markscheme: The shared electron pair is split equally between the two chlorine atoms <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the covalent bond
steps.content: To understand the initiation step, we first look at the chlorine molecule, $Cl_2$. The two chlorine atoms are held together by a **single covalent bond**, which consists of a pair of shared electrons ($Cl : Cl$).

steps.title: Define homolytic fission
steps.content: During the initiation stage of a radical chain reaction (usually triggered by UV light), the $Cl-Cl$ bond undergoes **homolytic fission**. In chemistry, "homo-" means same or equal, and "fission" means splitting. This concept is covered in **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Describe the electron movement
steps.content:
In this specific type of bond breaking, the shared pair of electrons is **split equally**. Each chlorine atom takes one electron from the original shared pair. 

We often represent this using "fish-hook" arrows (single-headed arrows) to show the movement of a single electron to each atom.

highlights.text: The shared electron pair is split equally between the two chlorine atoms

steps.title: State the result
steps.content:
Because each chlorine atom now has an unpaired valence electron, they become neutral but highly reactive species called **radicals** ($Cl\bullet$).

$$\text{Cl}-\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{UV} \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet$$

parts.content: Identify the type of arrow used to represent movement of a single electron.
parts.markscheme: A single-headed (fish-hook) arrow <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Recall electron movement notation
steps.content: In organic chemistry mechanisms, we use curved arrows to show the movement of electrons. Most reactions involve the movement of an electron **pair**, which is represented by a double-headed arrow. However, when dealing with radicals (like the chlorine radical mentioned in the question), we are often tracking the movement of just **one** electron.

highlights.text: Chlorine radicals can initiate a chain reaction.

steps.title: Identify single electron notation
steps.content:
To distinguish the movement of a single electron from an electron pair, we use a specific notation. This arrow has only one 'barb' or head. 

In IB Chemistry, this is formally identified as a **single-headed arrow** or, more commonly, a **fish-hook arrow**. 

You will encounter this frequently in **Topic R3.3: Radicals** when studying homolytic fission or propagation steps in radical substitution reactions.

steps.title: Final identification
steps.content: The correct terminology required for this mark is either "single-headed arrow" or "fish-hook arrow". Both terms are accepted in the markscheme to describe the movement of a single electron.

parts.content: Write the overall equation for the reaction between methane and chlorine.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{CH_4 + Cl_2 \to CH_3Cl + HCl}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content:
The question asks for the reaction between methane and chlorine. Methane is the simplest alkane, and chlorine exists as a diatomic molecule.

- Methane: $CH_4$
- Chlorine: $Cl_2$

steps.title: Apply substitution logic
steps.content:
In a free-radical substitution reaction, one hydrogen atom from the alkane is replaced by one halogen atom. 

Starting with $CH_4$, if we remove one hydrogen atom ($H$), we are left with a methyl group ($-CH_3$). We then add one chlorine atom ($Cl$) to this group to form the first product, chloromethane ($CH_3Cl$).

steps.title: Identify the by-product
steps.content: The hydrogen atom that was removed from the methane must bond with the remaining chlorine atom from the $Cl_2$ molecule. This results in the formation of hydrogen chloride ($HCl$) as a by-product.

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Now we combine the reactants and products into a balanced chemical equation. This reaction typically requires UV light as a condition to initiate the radical mechanism.

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$$

steps.title: Identify the propagation steps
steps.content:
In the free-radical substitution of alkanes, the overall reaction is the sum of the two propagation steps. These steps represent a cycle where radical intermediates are consumed and then regenerated. 

This process is covered in **Reactivity 3.3: Substitution reactions with alkanes**. To find the overall equation, we first need to write out these two specific steps.

steps.title: First propagation step
steps.content:
In the first propagation step, a chlorine radical ($Cl\cdot$) attacks a methane molecule ($CH_4$). This results in the formation of hydrogen chloride and a methyl radical ($\cdot CH_3$):

$$\text{Step 1: } CH_4 + Cl\cdot \to \cdot CH_3 + HCl$$

steps.title: Second propagation step
steps.content:
In the second propagation step, the methyl radical ($\cdot CH_3$) produced in the first step reacts with a new chlorine molecule ($Cl_2$). This forms the halogenoalkane product and regenerates the chlorine radical:

$$\text{Step 2: } \cdot CH_3 + Cl_2 \to CH_3Cl + Cl\cdot$$

steps.title: Sum the equations
steps.content:
To get the overall equation, we add the two steps together and cancel out any species that appear on both sides (the radical intermediates):

$$\begin{aligned} &CH_4 + \cancel{Cl\cdot} \to \cancel{\cdot CH_3} + HCl \\ + &\cancel{\cdot CH_3} + Cl_2 \to CH_3Cl + \cancel{Cl\cdot} \\ \hline &CH_4 + Cl_2 \to CH_3Cl + HCl \end{aligned}$$

Notice how the $Cl\cdot$ and $\cdot CH_3$ cancel out because they act as catalysts/intermediates in the cycle.

steps.title: Final overall equation
steps.content:
The final balanced equation for the substitution of methane with chlorine is:

$$CH_4 + Cl_2 \to CH_3Cl + HCl$$

parts.content: State the type of reaction this represents.
parts.markscheme:
- Radical substitution <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify key terms
steps.content:
To name the reaction type, we look directly at the information provided in the question. The prompt specifies two key characteristics of the process:
1. The chemical transformation is a **substitution**.
2. The process occurs via a **radical** mechanism.

steps.title: Combine descriptors
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, we name reaction types by combining the mechanism style with the nature of the change. By pairing the mechanism descriptor with the reaction class, we get the formal identification.

$$\text{Radical} + \text{Substitution} = \text{Radical substitution}$$

steps.title: State the final type
steps.content: This reaction is formally known as **radical substitution**. This concept is covered in **Topic R3.3: Substitution reactions with alkanes**, where you learn that halogens react with alkanes in the presence of UV light to create halogenoalkanes through this specific mechanism.

parts.content: Explain why multiple substitution products can form in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Once $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ is formed, it can react further with $\mathrm{Cl}\bullet$ <marks>A1</marks>
- This leads to further substitutions like $\mathrm{CH_2Cl_2}$, $\mathrm{CHCl_3}$, $\mathrm{CCl_4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the first product
steps.content:
In the radical substitution of an alkane like methane ($\text{CH}_4$), the first propagation cycle produces chloromethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$). While this is a stable molecule, it is not the "end" of the reaction. 

This process is covered in **Topic R3.3: Substitution reactions with alkanes**. To understand this step, it is helpful to remember that propagation steps are cyclic and generate new radicals that keep the reaction going.

steps.title: Identify remaining C-H bonds
steps.content:
The key to multiple substitutions is that the initial product, $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$, still contains three **C-H bonds**. 

Because chlorine radicals ($\text{Cl}\bullet$) are highly reactive, they do not distinguish between the $\text{C-H}$ bonds in the original methane and the $\text{C-H}$ bonds in the newly formed chloromethane. This means the chloromethane itself can become a reactant in a new propagation cycle.

highlights.text: Once $\mathrm{CH_3Cl}$ is formed, it can react further with $\mathrm{Cl}\bullet$

steps.title: Successive propagation steps
steps.content:
When a chlorine radical attacks $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$, it triggers a **successive propagation step**. The process looks like this:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\bullet + \text{HCl}$$
$$\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\bullet + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Cl}\bullet$$

Here, $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ (dichloromethane) is formed. This sequence can repeat as long as there are hydrogen atoms left on the carbon atom to be replaced.

steps.title: The range of products
steps.content:
Because these steps can happen repeatedly, a mixture of products is formed. Each successive step replaces one more hydrogen with a chlorine atom until the carbon is fully saturated with halogens.

The possible products include:
- **Dichloromethane**: $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$
- **Trichloromethane** (chloroform): $\text{CHCl}_3$
- **Tetrachloromethane**: $\text{CCl}_4$

To maximize the yield of just one product, chemists must carefully control the ratio of the alkane to the halogen.

highlights.text: This leads to further substitutions like $\mathrm{CH_2Cl_2}$, $\mathrm{CHCl_3}$, $\mathrm{CCl_4}$

parts.content: Write one possible propagation step for the reaction of $\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$ with $\text{CH}_4$.
parts.markscheme: $\text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_4 \to \text{HCl} + \text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$ <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content:
In a radical substitution reaction, a propagation step involves a radical reacting with a stable molecule to produce a new radical and a new molecule. Based on the question, our reactants are the chlorine radical ($Cl^{\bullet}$) and methane ($CH_4$).

This process is part of **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Apply hydrogen abstraction
steps.content:
The chlorine radical is highly reactive. It undergoes **hydrogen abstraction**, where it pulls one hydrogen atom away from the methane molecule. 

When the $Cl^{\bullet}$ radical gains a hydrogen atom, it forms a stable covalent bond, resulting in hydrogen chloride ($HCl$).

steps.title: Identify the new radical
steps.content:
Because the methane ($CH_4$) lost one hydrogen atom, it is left with an unpaired electron. This transforms the methane molecule into a **methyl radical** ($CH_3^{\bullet}$).

Notice that we started with one radical ($Cl^{\bullet}$) and ended with one radical ($CH_3^{\bullet}$), which is the defining characteristic of a propagation step.

steps.title: Formulate the final equation
steps.content:
By combining the reactants and products, we get the balanced propagation equation:

$$Cl^{\bullet} + CH_4 \to HCl + CH_3^{\bullet}$$

This equation earns the 1 mark available for showing the correct species and the conservation of the radical state.

parts.content: Write the second propagation step that regenerates a chlorine radical.
parts.markscheme: $\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}_2 \to \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$ <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction stage
steps.content: In the study of organic chemistry (**Topic R3.3.3: Substitution reactions with alkanes**), we learn that the reaction between alkanes and halogens occurs via a free-radical substitution mechanism. This mechanism consists of three stages: initiation, propagation, and termination. We are specifically looking for the second part of the propagation stage.

steps.title: Recall the first propagation
steps.content:
To find the second step, it helps to recall the first. In the first propagation step, a chlorine radical ($\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$) reacts with the alkane (methane, in this case) to produce an alkyl radical and hydrogen chloride:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{Cl}^{\bullet} \to \text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{HCl}$$

The methyl radical ($\text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$) produced here becomes the reactant for our target step.

steps.title: Formulate the second step
steps.content:
In the second propagation step, the methyl radical ($\text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$) reacts with a chlorine molecule ($\text{Cl}_2$). This step is crucial because it forms the halogenated product and, importantly, regenerates the chlorine radical ($\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$) so the chain reaction can continue.

Following the prompt to "regenerate a chlorine radical":

$$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}_2 \to \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

steps.title: Final Equation
steps.content:
Ensure you include the dots to represent the unpaired electrons on the radicals. The complete equation for the second propagation step is:

$$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}_2 \to \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

steps.title: Identify the intermediate
steps.content:
In a radical chain reaction like the chlorination of methane, the first propagation step produces an intermediate alkyl radical. For methane ($\text{CH}_4$), this intermediate is the methyl radical, $\text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$. 

This concept is part of **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**, specifically regarding substitution reactions with alkanes.

steps.title: Apply propagation definition
steps.content:
A **propagation step** is defined by having one radical as a reactant and generating a new radical as a product. This keeps the chain reaction going. 

Since we are in the second step, our reactant radical is the product of the first step: $\text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$.

steps.title: Deduce the reactants
steps.content: To regenerate a chlorine radical ($\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$), the methyl radical must react with a stable chlorine molecule ($\text{Cl}_2$) from the reaction mixture.

steps.title: Construct the final equation
steps.content:
When the methyl radical reacts with the chlorine molecule, one $\text{Cl}$ atom bonds to the methyl group to form chloromethane, and the other $\text{Cl}$ atom is released as a radical:

$$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}_2 \to \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

This completes the cycle by providing the $\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$ needed for the first propagation step to occur again.

parts.content: Explain why chain reactions continue once started.
parts.markscheme:
Propagation steps produce radicals <marks>[1]</marks>
These radicals react further to sustain the chain <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the propagation phase
steps.content: To understand why a chain reaction continues, we look at the **propagation** stage. In this stage, a highly reactive radical (produced during initiation) reacts with a stable, neutral molecule. This concept is covered in **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Formation of new radicals
steps.content:
The key to the chain continuing is that the reaction between a radical and a neutral molecule doesn't just produce a product; it also produces a **new radical**. 

For example, in the chlorination of methane:
$$\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{HCl} + \text{CH}_3^\bullet$$

Here, the chlorine radical is consumed, but a methyl radical is created. This earns the first mark for recognizing that propagation steps produce radicals.

steps.title: Sustaining the cycle
steps.content:
Because a new radical is generated, it is free to react with another neutral molecule, starting the process all over again:
$$\text{CH}_3^\bullet + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^\bullet$$

This creates a **self-sustaining cycle** (or 'chain') where the product of one step is the reactant for the next. This earns the second mark for explaining how these radicals react further to sustain the chain.

highlights.text: These radicals react further to sustain the chain

steps.title: Focus on propagation
steps.content:
To understand why a chain reaction continues, we need to look at the **propagation** phase. In this stage, a radical reacts with a stable molecule to produce a *new* radical. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**, specifically when looking at the substitution reactions of alkanes.

steps.title: First propagation step
steps.content:
Let's use the chlorination of methane as our example. In the first propagation step, a chlorine radical ($\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$) reacts with a methane molecule:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{Cl}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{HCl}$$

Notice that while the chlorine radical is used up, a new **methyl radical** ($\text{CH}_3^{\bullet}$) is produced. This satisfies the first part of the markscheme: propagation steps produce radicals.

steps.title: Radical regeneration
steps.content:
The reaction doesn't stop there because the new methyl radical is highly reactive. It immediately reacts with another chlorine molecule:

$$\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

In this step, the original **chlorine radical** ($\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$) is regenerated. Because this radical is back in the system, it can go right back to Step 1 and start the process all over again.

steps.title: Sustaining the chain
steps.content: The reason the reaction continues is that the product of one step is the reactant for the next. This creates a cycle where the **radicals react further to sustain the chain**, allowing thousands of substitutions to occur from a single initiation event.

highlights.text: These radicals react further to sustain the chain

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content: To answer this question, we need to provide the precise chemical definition of a radical. In the IB Chemistry syllabus, this is covered under **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**, specifically section **R3.3.1 Radicals**.

highlights.text: State what is meant by the term radical.

steps.title: Apply the direct definition
steps.content:
A radical is defined as an atom, molecule, or ion that contains at least one **unpaired electron**. 

In chemical notation, we usually represent this unpaired electron with a single dot next to the chemical symbol. For example, a chlorine radical is written as:

$$Cl^{\bullet}$$

highlights.text: A species with an unpaired electron

**Final Answer:** A radical is a species with an unpaired electron.

steps.title: Determine total electrons
steps.content:
To find the electron configuration, we first need to know how many electrons are in a neutral chlorine atom. Looking at the periodic table, chlorine (Cl) has an atomic number of 17, which means it has 17 electrons.

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom** and **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

steps.title: Write the full configuration
steps.content:
Using the Aufbau principle, we fill the subshells in order of increasing energy ($1s$, $2s$, $2p$, $3s$, $3p$):

$$\text{Cl: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5$$

We can verify the total number of electrons: $2 + 2 + 6 + 2 + 5 = 17$.

steps.title: Analyze the 3p subshell
steps.content:
All inner subshells ($1s, 2s, 2p, 3s$) are completely full, so all their electrons are paired. We only need to look at the highest energy subshell, the $3p^5$ subshell, to find unpaired electrons.

A $p$ subshell contains 3 orbitals. According to Hund's rule and the Pauli exclusion principle, we distribute the 5 electrons as follows:
1. Place one electron in each of the three orbitals (3 electrons used).
2. Pair up the remaining 2 electrons in the first two orbitals.

$$\begin{matrix} \uparrow\downarrow & \uparrow\downarrow & \uparrow \\ \text{orbital 1} & \text{orbital 2} & \text{orbital 3} \end{matrix}$$

This results in two full orbitals and one orbital with a single electron.

steps.title: Identify unpaired electrons
steps.content:
From our orbital diagram of the $3p^5$ subshell, we can see there is exactly one electron that does not have a pair. 

By definition, a radical is a species with at least one unpaired electron. Therefore, the chlorine atom radical has **one unpaired electron**.

highlights.text: Identify the number of unpaired electrons: One unpaired electron

steps.title: Locate Chlorine in the Periodic Table
steps.content:
To find the number of valence electrons, we first look at the position of Chlorine ($Cl$) in the periodic table. Chlorine is found in **Group 17** (the halogens). 

According to periodic trends in **Topic S3.1**, the group number tells us the number of electrons in the outer shell for main-group elements.

steps.title: Determine valence electrons
steps.content:
Elements in Group 17 have **7 valence electrons** in their outermost energy level. 

For a neutral chlorine atom, these 7 electrons are what we use to determine its bonding behavior and radical nature.

steps.title: Arrange electrons (Lewis approach)
steps.content:
Using the Valence Shell Analysis (Lewis dot approach), we place the 7 electrons around the $Cl$ symbol. 

1. We place one electron on each of the four sides (top, bottom, left, right).
2. We then pair the remaining electrons.

Since we have 7 electrons:
$$\text{Pairs} = \text{floor}\left(\frac{7}{2}\right) = 3 \text{ pairs}$$
$$\text{Unpaired} = 7 - (3 \times 2) = 1 \text{ unpaired electron}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 7 minus the total electrons in pairs (3 pairs of 2).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the remaining single electron.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of unpaired electrons by finding the remainder when 7 electrons are paired.

steps.title: Identify the radical species
steps.content: In chemistry, a **radical** is defined as a species with at least one unpaired electron. Based on our valence shell analysis, a chlorine atom radical has exactly **one** unpaired electron.

steps.title: Define a radical
steps.content: By definition, a **radical** is a highly reactive chemical species that contains at least one **unpaired electron**. In IB Chemistry, you encounter these species in **Topic R3.3.1**, where they are often represented with a single dot ($\,^{\bullet}$) to signify the lone electron.

steps.title: Analyze homolytic fission
steps.content:
Chlorine radicals are formed through **homolytic fission** of a chlorine molecule ($Cl_2$). 

In a covalent bond, two atoms share a pair of electrons. During homolytic fission, this bond breaks such that each chlorine atom retains exactly **one** of the two shared electrons from the bond. This is covered in **Topic R3.3.2**.

steps.title: Determine unpaired electrons
steps.content:
When the $Cl-Cl$ bond breaks, the shared pair is split. Because each chlorine atom takes only one electron from that formerly shared pair, that electron is now sitting alone in an orbital.

Therefore, a chlorine atom radical possesses **one unpaired electron**.

steps.content: To find the number of valence electrons, we first locate Chlorine ($\text{Cl}$) in the periodic table. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table: Classification of elements**. Chlorine is found in Group 17 (the halogens).

steps.title: Determine valence electrons
steps.content:
For main-group elements, the number of valence electrons corresponds to the last digit of the group number. Since Chlorine is in Group 17, it has 7 electrons in its outer shell.

$$17 - 10 = 7 \text{ valence electrons}$$

steps.title: Arrange the electrons
steps.content:
In a dot-and-cross diagram, valence electrons are represented as dots or crosses around the element's symbol. For a radical, which is a species with an unpaired electron (see **Topic R3.3.1: Radicals**), we arrange the 7 electrons as three pairs and one single, unpaired electron.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total electrons} &= 7 \\ \text{Paired electrons} &= 3 \times 2 = 6 \\ \text{Unpaired electrons} &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: three pairs and one unpaired electron

steps.title: Draw the radical
steps.content:
Place the symbol $\text{Cl}$ in the center. Surround it with three pairs of electrons (represented as dots or crosses) and one single electron to represent the radical state.

$$ :\overset{\large..}{\underset{\large..}{\text{Cl}}} \cdot $$

steps.title: Determine the atomic number
steps.content:
To draw a full shell representation, we first need to know how many electrons a neutral chlorine atom has. Looking at the periodic table, chlorine (Cl) has an atomic number of 17.

Since a radical is a neutral species with an unpaired electron, we will be placing exactly 17 electrons in our diagram.

steps.title: Calculate electron shell distribution
steps.content:
Using the Bohr model of main energy levels (shells), we distribute the 17 electrons from the nucleus outward:

- **First shell**: holds a maximum of 2 electrons.
- **Second shell**: holds a maximum of 8 electrons.
- **Third (valence) shell**: holds the remaining electrons.

$$\begin{aligned} 17 - (2 + 8) &= 7 \text{ electrons} \end{aligned}$$

So, the electron configuration is **2, 8, 7**.

steps.title: Draw the full shells
steps.content:
In the **Full Shell Representation**, we draw the nucleus in the center and three concentric circles to represent the main energy levels:

1.  **Inner Circle**: Place 2 electrons (usually as a pair).
2.  **Middle Circle**: Place 8 electrons (usually as 4 pairs).
3.  **Outer Circle**: Place 7 electrons.

steps.title: Represent the radical electron
steps.content:
A radical is defined by having an unpaired electron. In your drawing of the outer shell (7 electrons), you should group them as **three pairs and one single, unpaired electron**.

To earn the mark, ensure your diagram clearly shows:
- 3 full shells.
- 7 electrons in the valence shell.
- One of those 7 electrons is single (the radical dot $\bullet$).

steps.title: Analyze the bond-breaking mechanism
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, when a covalent bond breaks, the two electrons in the shared pair must be redistributed. The question describes a specific type of breaking where the bond breaks **symmetrically**, meaning each atom involved in the bond retains exactly **one electron** from the original pair.

This is represented as:
$$\text{X—Y} \rightarrow \text{X}^\bullet + \text{Y}^\bullet$$

In the case of a chlorine molecule ($$Cl_2$$), it looks like this:
$$\text{Cl—Cl} \xrightarrow{UV} \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet$$

steps.title: Identify the chemical term
steps.content:
We use specific terminology to describe this equal splitting of a bond:

*   **Homo-**: Meaning "the same" or "equal."
*   **-lytic/Fission**: Meaning "splitting" or "breaking."

This term is found in **Reactivity 3.3.2** of your syllabus. Since each atom ends up with an unpaired electron, they are called **free radicals**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The standard chemical term for the symmetrical breaking of a covalent bond into two radicals is **homolytic fission**. 

In the IB exam, providing this exact term is necessary to earn the mark.

steps.title: Understand homolytic fission
steps.content: In **Topic R3.3 (Electron sharing reactions)**, we learn that **homolytic fission** occurs when a covalent bond breaks and each of the bonded atoms takes one of the shared electrons. This process results in the formation of highly reactive species called **radicals**.

steps.title: Identify the reactant
steps.content: Using the **Molecular Formula Approach**, we represent the starting material as a diatomic chlorine molecule, which has the formula $\text{Cl}_2$. This molecule contains a single covalent bond ($ \text{Cl}-\text{Cl} $) that will be broken during the reaction.

steps.title: Represent the radicals
steps.content: When the bond in $\text{Cl}_2$ breaks homolytically (usually initiated by UV light), it produces two separate chlorine atoms. Because each atom retains one electron from the original shared pair, they are now radicals. We represent this unpaired electron using a single dot $(\bullet)$ next to the element symbol.

highlights.text: Dot denotes unpaired electron on each Cl atom.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
Now, we combine the reactant and the products into a balanced chemical equation. Since one molecule of chlorine gas produces two chlorine radicals, the equation is:

$$\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

steps.title: Define homolytic fission
steps.content:
To answer this, we first need to understand what **homolytic fission** is. In this process, a covalent bond breaks in a way that the shared pair of electrons is split equally. Each atom involved in the bond takes one electron, resulting in the formation of two **radicals**.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Represent the chlorine bond
steps.content:
Using the structural representation approach, we start by looking at the $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ molecule. It consists of two chlorine atoms held together by a single covalent bond:

$$\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$$

This line represents a shared pair of two electrons. To show where the fission occurs, we focus on this specific bond.

steps.title: Distribute the electrons
steps.content:
During homolytic fission, the bond breaks right down the middle. One electron from the shared pair moves to the left chlorine atom, and the other moves to the right chlorine atom. 

This leaves us with two separate chlorine atoms, each possessing a single, unpaired electron. In chemistry, we represent this unpaired electron with a **dot**.

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Now we combine these observations into a chemical equation. We start with the chlorine molecule and end with two chlorine radicals ($2\mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}$).

$$\mathrm{Cl}_2 \to 2\mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}$$

According to the markscheme, the dot denotes the unpaired electron on each Cl atom. Note that sometimes you might see the reactant drawn as $\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$ to show the bond breaking, but $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is the standard molecular notation.

highlights.text: Dot denotes unpaired electron on each Cl atom.

steps.title: Identify the initiation process
steps.content: The formation of radicals from chlorine molecules ($Cl_2$) occurs during the **initiation** stage of a free-radical substitution reaction. To split the stable chlorine molecule into two highly reactive chlorine radicals ($Cl\bullet$), energy must be supplied to break the covalent bond.

highlights.text: formation of radicals from chlorine molecules

steps.title: State the required condition
steps.content:
The specific condition required to initiate this process is **ultraviolet (UV) light**. 

In the context of photochemical initiation, UV light provides the necessary energy to trigger the reaction at room temperature.

steps.title: Explain homolytic fission
steps.content:
UV light consists of photons with a specific energy determined by the equation:

$$E = hf$$

This photon energy is sufficient to cause **homolytic fission** of the $Cl-Cl$ bond. In this process, the shared pair of electrons is split equally, with one electron going to each chlorine atom, resulting in the formation of two radicals:

$$Cl_2 \xrightarrow{UV} 2Cl\bullet$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state that **ultraviolet light** (or UV light) is required. 

This provides the energy for the homolytic fission of the chlorine-chlorine bond.

steps.title: Identify the chemical process
steps.content:
The formation of radicals from chlorine molecules ($Cl_2$) is the **initiation** step of a radical substitution reaction. In this step, the covalent bond between the two chlorine atoms must break to produce two separate chlorine radicals:

$$Cl_2 \rightarrow 2Cl\cdot$$

This process is known as **homolytic fission**, where each chlorine atom takes one electron from the shared bonding pair.

highlights.text: formation of radicals from chlorine molecules

steps.title: Analyze energy requirements
steps.content: Breaking the $Cl-Cl$ bond requires an input of energy equal to or greater than the **bond enthalpy**. According to **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**, this energy is needed to overcome the attractive forces holding the atoms together in the molecule.

steps.title: Apply Thermal Initiation approach
steps.content:
Using the **thermal initiation** approach, we identify that this energy can be supplied in the form of **heat** (or high temperatures). 

Providing thermal energy increases the vibrational energy of the $Cl-Cl$ bond until it reaches the point of dissociation, resulting in the formation of radicals.

steps.title: State the final condition
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, either UV light or heat is acceptable. For this specific approach, the condition is:

**Heat** (or high temperature).

highlights.text: Ultraviolet light or heat is required

steps.title: Identify the mechanism stage
steps.content: Alkanes like methane ($\mathrm{CH}_4$) react with halogens like bromine ($\mathrm{Br}_2$) through a process called **free-radical substitution**. This mechanism consists of three main stages: initiation, propagation, and termination. The question specifically asks for the **initiation step**.

steps.title: Explain homolytic fission
steps.content: In the initiation stage, the covalent bond between the two bromine atoms in $\mathrm{Br}_2$ breaks. Because both atoms have the same electronegativity, the electron pair is split equally, with each atom taking one electron. This is known as **homolytic fission** and results in the formation of two highly reactive **bromine radicals** ($\mathrm{Br}^\bullet$).

steps.title: Determine the required condition

Combining these parts, we write the balanced chemical equation for the initiation step. We use a dot ($\bullet$) to represent the unpaired electron on the bromine radicals:

$$\mathrm{Br}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{UV}} 2\mathrm{Br}^\bullet$$

This single equation earns the mark by showing the reactant, the condition, and the correct radical products.

steps.title: Identify radical intermediates
steps.content:
In the radical substitution reaction between methane ($CH_4$) and bromine ($Br_2$), two key radical intermediates are formed during the initiation and propagation stages:

1.  **Bromine radicals**: $Br^\bullet$
2.  **Methyl radicals**: $CH_3^\bullet$

To write a termination step, we simply need to show what happens when any two of these radicals collide and bond together.

steps.title: Understand the termination process
steps.content: A termination step occurs when two radicals react to form a stable molecule. Since radicals have unpaired electrons, they are highly reactive. When they meet, they pair up their electrons to form a covalent bond, which effectively removes the radicals from the system and stops the chain reaction.

steps.title: Combine available radicals
steps.content:
Using the **Radical Combination Method**, we can pair the radicals we identified in Step 0 in three different ways. You only need to provide two for full marks:

**Option 1: Two bromine radicals combine**
$$Br^\bullet + Br^\bullet \to Br_2$$

**Option 2: A methyl radical and a bromine radical combine**
$$CH_3^\bullet + Br^\bullet \to CH_3Br$$

**Option 3: Two methyl radicals combine**
$$CH_3^\bullet + CH_3^\bullet \to C_2H_6$$

Each correct equation earns 1 mark.

steps.title: Identify the reaction stage
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, the reaction between alkanes like methane ($\mathrm{CH}_4$) and halogens like bromine ($\mathrm{Br}_2$) follows a **free radical substitution** mechanism. This process consists of three distinct stages: **initiation**, **propagation**, and **termination**. 

You can review these mechanisms in **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: Explain what is meant by a termination step.

steps.title: Define the termination step
steps.content:
A **termination step** is defined as a chemical reaction where two free radicals collide and combine. 

Free radicals are highly reactive species because they contain an unpaired electron. When two of these radicals meet, they share their unpaired electrons to form a new, stable covalent bond.

steps.title: Explain the outcome
steps.content:
This combination results in the formation of a **stable non-radical molecule**. 

Because the radicals are consumed in this step and no new radicals are produced to replace them, the propagation of the chain reaction is effectively ended. 

An example of a termination step in this reaction is:
$$\mathrm{CH}_3\cdot + \mathrm{Br}\cdot \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{Br}$$

steps.title: Define the termination process
steps.content: In a free-radical substitution reaction, the **termination step** is the final stage of the mechanism. It occurs when two radicals (highly reactive species with unpaired electrons) collide and react with each other.

steps.title: Provide a specific example
steps.content:
Using the methane-bromine system as our example, a termination step involves the combination of a bromine radical and a methyl radical to form bromomethane:

$$\text{Br}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{CH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Br}$$

In this specific reaction, the two unpaired electrons from the radicals pair up to form a stable covalent bond in a non-radical product.

steps.title: Explain the outcome
steps.content: This step is called "termination" because it **ends the chain reaction**. Since two radicals are consumed and no new radicals are produced, there are no reactive species left to continue the propagation cycle.

highlights.text: A termination step occurs when two radicals combine to form a stable product, ending the chain reaction.

steps.title: Understand homolytic fission
steps.content:
In organic chemistry, **homolytic fission** occurs when a covalent bond breaks and the two electrons in the shared pair are divided equally between the two atoms. This process results in the formation of **radicals**, which are highly reactive species with an unpaired electron. 

You can review this concept in **Topic R3.3.2: Homolytic fission** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the electron movement
steps.content:
To represent the movement of a **single electron**, we must use a **single-headed arrow**, often called a "fish-hook" arrow. 

A standard double-headed arrow ($\curvearrowright$) represents the movement of an electron pair, which is used in heterolytic fission. For homolytic fission, we must use the fish-hook version ($\rightharpoonup$ or $\leftharpoonup$).

steps.title: Draw the first arrow
steps.content:
Start by identifying the $\text{Br}-\text{Br}$ covalent bond as a shared pair of electrons. 

Place the tail of the first fish-hook arrow exactly in the **center** of the bond. Point the head of the arrow towards the bromine atom on the left. This represents one electron from the shared pair moving to that atom.

highlights.text: Show one single-headed arrow starting from the Br-Br bond and pointing towards one Br atom

steps.title: Draw the second arrow
steps.content:
Now, draw a second fish-hook arrow. Again, start the tail from the **center** of the same bond, but point the head towards the bromine atom on the right. 

By showing two arrows originating from the bond and pointing to opposite atoms, you are demonstrating that the bond is splitting equally.

highlights.text: Show a second single-headed arrow starting from the Br-Br bond and pointing towards the other Br atom

steps.title: Finalize the mechanism
steps.content:
The final drawing should show the $\text{Br}-\text{Br}$ molecule with two fish-hook arrows curving away from the bond center towards each $\text{Br}$ atom. 

$$\text{Br} \mathrel{\unicode{x21bc}} \text{---} \mathrel{\unicode{x21c0}} \text{Br} \xrightarrow{uv} 2\text{Br}^\bullet$$

According to the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for each correctly placed fish-hook arrow. Ensure they originate from the bond and are clearly single-headed!

steps.title: Identify the bond type
steps.content:
We are looking at the fission (breaking) of a bromine molecule, $Br_2$. A bromine molecule consists of two bromine atoms held together by a single covalent bond, which contains two shared electrons.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Interpret the fish-hook arrows
steps.content:
In organic chemistry mechanisms, we use different types of arrows to show electron movement. A **fish-hook arrow** (a single-barbed arrow) represents the movement of a **single electron**.

When we see two fish-hook arrows starting from the center of a $Br-Br$ bond and pointing toward each individual atom, it tells us that the electron pair is being split equally.

highlights.text: Radicals can be represented with dot notation and fish-hook arrows.

steps.title: Apply homolytic fission
steps.content: Because each bromine atom takes exactly one electron from the shared pair, the bond undergoes **homolytic fission**. This process results in two neutral species that each possess an unpaired valence electron.

steps.title: Write the final products
steps.content:
Species with an unpaired electron are called **radicals** and are represented using a dot $(\cdot)$. 

Since the original $Br_2$ molecule split into two identical parts, the products are two bromine radicals:

$$2\text{Br}^{\cdot}$$

You can also write this as $\text{Br}^{\cdot} + \text{Br}^{\cdot}$.

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content: This question asks for the products of the fission of a bromine molecule during radical substitution. This specific process is known as **initiation**. It occurs when a covalent bond breaks in such a way that each atom from the bond takes one of the shared electrons.

steps.title: Apply homolytic fission
steps.content:
In **homolytic fission**, the $Br-Br$ bond breaks. Since both atoms are identical, they have the same electronegativity, and the bond splits equally. 

We represent the movement of single electrons using "fish-hook" (single-headed) arrows. Each bromine atom ends up with an unpaired valence electron, making it a radical. This process is covered in **Topic R3.3: Radicals** of your syllabus.

steps.title: State the final products
steps.content:
Because the original molecule contained two bromine atoms, the fission results in two identical bromine radicals. Following the IB markscheme, we represent these using dot notation:

$$2\text{Br}^{\cdot}$$

You may also write this as $\text{Br}^{\cdot} + \text{Br}^{\cdot}$. Both versions clearly show the unpaired electron (the dot) on the bromine atoms.

parts.content: State the role of light in this process.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that light (UV) provides the energy required to break the covalent bond homolytically <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the reaction stage
steps.content: In organic chemistry, specifically in **Topic R3.3: Electron sharing reactions**, we learn that the formation of radicals occurs during the **initiation** step of a reaction. This step requires an external source of energy to start the process of breaking bonds.

steps.title: Define the energy source
steps.content:
Light, particularly in the **ultraviolet (UV)** spectrum, possesses enough energy to be absorbed by certain covalent bonds (like the $Cl-Cl$ bond in chlorine gas). 

This energy is used to overcome the bond enthalpy, which is the energy required to break the bond.

highlights.text: Identify that light (UV) provides the energy required to break the covalent bond homolytically

steps.title: Explain homolytic fission
steps.content:
When the bond breaks using this light energy, it undergoes **homolytic fission**. This means the shared pair of electrons in the covalent bond is split equally, with one electron moving to each atom to form two radicals.

$$\text{Cl}-\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{UV} \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The role of light (specifically UV radiation) is to provide the energy required to break the covalent bond homolytically to generate radicals.

parts.content: Identify the homologous series to which ethyl butanoate belongs.
parts.markscheme: Esters <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the molecular structure
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we first need to look at the functional group present in the provided structure of ethyl butanoate. This is a key skill covered in **S3.2.2: Functional groups**.

Looking at the center of the molecule, we see a carbon atom double-bonded to one oxygen atom ($C=O$) and single-bonded to another oxygen atom ($-O-$), which is then connected to a second carbon chain ($R'$).

The general structure looks like this:
$$R-C(=O)-O-R'$$

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The specific arrangement where a carbonyl group ($C=O$) is directly adjacent to an ether linkage ($-O-$) is known as the **ester** functional group, often written as **-COO-**.

In this specific molecule:
- The group on the left ($R$) is a propyl group ($-C_3H_7$).
- The group on the right ($R'$) is an ethyl group ($-C_2H_5$).
- The central link is the ester group.

highlights.text: Identify the homologous series to which ethyl butanoate belongs.

steps.title: Determine the homologous series
steps.content:
Any organic compound containing the $-COO-$ functional group belongs to the homologous series called **esters**. 

You can also recognize this from the name "ethyl butano**ate**". The suffix **-oate** is a standard IUPAC convention for naming members of the ester series.

steps.title: Analyze the IUPAC name
steps.content:
To identify the homologous series, we examine the IUPAC name provided: **ethyl butanoate**. 

In organic chemistry, names are constructed using specific prefixes and suffixes that correspond to different functional groups. This is a core concept covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Identify the characteristic suffix
steps.content:
Looking at the ending of the name "butanoate", we identify the suffix **-oate**.

According to IUPAC nomenclature rules, the **-oate** suffix is the standard designation for the **ester** homologous series. In these compounds, the name consists of two parts:
1. The alkyl group attached to the oxygen (ethyl).
2. The carboxylate group derived from the parent acid (butanoate).

steps.title: State the homologous series
steps.content:
Since the suffix **-oate** is unique to this functional group, we can conclude that the compound belongs to the **esters**.

Award 1 mark for identifying the correct series as per the markscheme.

parts.content: State the functional group present in ethyl butanoate.
parts.markscheme: Ester group ($-\text{COO}-$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The question asks us to identify the specific functional group found in the molecule **ethyl butanoate**, based on the provided skeletal structure. In organic chemistry, functional groups are specific atoms or groups of atoms that determine the chemical properties of a molecule.

highlights.text: State the functional group present in ethyl butanoate.

steps.title: Perform structural analysis
steps.content:
Looking at the skeletal structure, we focus on the central part of the molecule where the oxygen atoms are located. We can see a **carbonyl group** ($C=O$) directly bonded to an **oxygen atom** which is then bonded to another carbon chain ($R-COO-R'$).

This specific arrangement, where a carbonyl is adjacent to an ether-like oxygen link, is the defining feature of a specific functional group.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
Based on **Topic S3.2.2: Functional groups**, the structure containing the $-\text{COO}-$ link between two carbon chains is known as an **ester**. 

In this molecule, the 'butanoate' part comes from the four-carbon chain attached to the carbonyl, and the 'ethyl' part comes from the two-carbon chain attached to the single-bonded oxygen.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The functional group present is the **ester** group.

To earn the mark, you can simply state "ester" or show the group formula as $-\text{COO}-$.

steps.title: Analyze the compound name
steps.content: To determine the functional group using nomenclature analysis, we first look closely at the IUPAC name provided in the question: **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Identify the suffix
steps.content:
In IUPAC organic chemistry nomenclature, the suffix (the ending of the name) tells us which functional group is the most important in the molecule. 

Looking at "butanoate", we can see the suffix is **-oate**.

steps.title: Map suffix to group
steps.content:
According to the rules covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**, the suffix **-oate** is specifically used for **esters**. 

An ester is formed when a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol, and its characteristic functional group is the carboxylate group: 

$$-\text{COO}-$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our nomenclature analysis of the **-oate** suffix, the functional group present is the **ester** group.

To get full marks, you can write the name "ester" or provide the structural formula.

**Final Answer:** Ester group ($-\text{COO}-$)

parts.content: Name the two organic compounds used to prepare ethyl butanoate.
parts.markscheme:
Butanoic acid <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ethanol <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the ester
steps.content:
The compound given is **ethyl butanoate**, which belongs to the **ester** functional group. Esters are formed through a condensation reaction (specifically esterification) between an alcohol and a carboxylic acid.

To identify the reactants, we can deconstruct the IUPAC name using nomenclature analysis. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

steps.title: Analyze the alcohol component
steps.content:
In the IUPAC name of an ester, the first word describes the alkyl group derived from the **alcohol**. 

The prefix **ethyl** indicates a two-carbon chain ($C_2$). Therefore, the alcohol used in this reaction is **ethanol**.

steps.title: Analyze the acid component
steps.content:
The second part of the name, ending in **-oate**, describes the part of the molecule derived from the **carboxylic acid**. 

The term **butanoate** stems from "butane," indicating a four-carbon chain ($C_4$). Therefore, the carboxylic acid used is **butanoic acid**.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
Based on our nomenclature analysis, the two organic compounds used to prepare ethyl butanoate are:

1.  **Ethanol** (1 mark)
2.  **Butanoic acid** (1 mark)

steps.title: Identify the ester group
steps.content:
First, we look at the structural formula provided. We can identify the **ester functional group**, which is represented by the $-COO-$ linkage in the middle of the molecule. 

Esters are formed through a condensation reaction between a **carboxylic acid** and an **alcohol**. To find the starting materials, we need to analyze which part of the molecule came from which reactant.

steps.title: Cleave the ester bond
steps.content:
Using the **Structural Analysis** approach, we 'cleave' (cut) the molecule at the specific bond formed during esterification. This bond is always located between the **carbonyl carbon** ($C=O$) and the **single-bonded oxygen** ($-O-$).

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(=\text{O}) \quad \bm{\text{|}} \quad \text{O}\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$$

Breaking the molecule here allows us to see the two precursor 'skeletons'.

steps.title: Identify the acid part
steps.content:
The part of the molecule containing the carbonyl group ($C=O$) comes from the **carboxylic acid**. 

Counting the carbons in this section:
1. Carbon 1 (the carbonyl carbon)
2. Carbon 2 (next $CH_2$)
3. Carbon 3 (next $CH_2$)
4. Carbon 4 (terminal $CH_3$)

A 4-carbon carboxylic acid is called **butanoic acid**. This corresponds to the 'butanoate' part of the name **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Identify the alcohol part
steps.content:
The part of the molecule attached to the single-bonded oxygen comes from the **alcohol**. 

Counting the carbons in this section:
1. Carbon 1 ($CH_2$)
2. Carbon 2 ($CH_3$)

A 2-carbon alcohol is called **ethanol**. This corresponds to the 'ethyl' part of the name **ethyl butanoate**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the carbon chains on either side of the ester linkage, the two organic compounds used are:

1. **Butanoic acid**
2. **Ethanol**

According to the markscheme, you receive 1 mark for each correct name.

parts.content: Write an equation for the formation of ethyl butanoate from these compounds, including structural formulas.

$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH} + \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOCH_2CH_3} + \mathrm{H_2O}$$

<marks>1 mark</marks> for correct reactants, <marks>1 mark</marks> for correct product

steps.title: Analyze the ester components
steps.content:
To write the equation for the formation of an ester, we first need to identify the two components that make it up: the **carboxylic acid** and the **alcohol**. 

In the name **ethyl butanoate**:
*   The prefix **'butanoate'** indicates the ester was derived from **butanoic acid** (a 4-carbon carboxylic acid).
*   The prefix **'ethyl'** indicates the ester was derived from **ethanol** (a 2-carbon alcohol).

steps.title: Identify the carboxylic acid
steps.content:
Butanoic acid is a straight-chain carboxylic acid with four carbon atoms. Using the **condensed structural approach**, we represent the alkyl chain followed by the carboxyl functional group ($-COOH$).

Its formula is:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$$

steps.title: Identify the alcohol
steps.content:
Ethanol is a two-carbon alcohol. We write its condensed structural formula showing the ethyl group attached to the hydroxyl group ($-OH$).

Its formula is:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

steps.title: Construct the balanced equation
steps.content:
Esterification is a **condensation reaction** that typically occurs in the presence of an acid catalyst. The carboxylic acid and alcohol react to form the ester and **water** ($H_2O$) as a byproduct. This reaction is reversible, so we use the equilibrium arrow ($\rightleftharpoons$).

Connecting the fragments, the final equation is:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for the correct reactants and **1 mark** for the correct products (the ester and water).

steps.title: Analyze the ester structure
steps.content:
To write the formation equation, we first need to identify the two components of the ester: the **carboxylic acid** and the **alcohol**. 

Looking at the name **ethyl butanoate**:
- The prefix **ethyl** comes from the alcohol (ethanol, 2 carbons).
- The suffix **butanoate** comes from the carboxylic acid (butanoic acid, 4 carbons).

You can also see this in the provided skeletal structure: the part of the molecule containing the $C=O$ group has a 4-carbon chain, while the part attached to the single oxygen atom has a 2-carbon chain.

steps.title: Identify the carboxylic acid
steps.content:
The "butanoate" portion corresponds to **butanoic acid**. In a skeletal structure, this is represented by a 4-carbon chain ending in a carboxyl group ($\text{-COOH}$).

Converting this skeletal visual into a condensed structural formula, we get:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$$

steps.title: Identify the alcohol
steps.content:
The "ethyl" portion corresponds to **ethanol**. Visually, this is a 2-carbon skeletal chain with a hydroxyl group ($\text{-OH}$) attached.

Converting this to a condensed structural formula, we get:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

steps.title: Write the condensation equation
steps.content:
In an esterification reaction, the carboxylic acid and alcohol react to form an ester and water. This is a reversible condensation reaction, so we use the equilibrium symbol ($\rightleftharpoons$).

Combine the reactants and products to form the final equation:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOCH}_2\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

As per the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the correct reactants and 1 mark for the correct products (including the water molecule!).

parts.content: Outline the type of reaction.
parts.markscheme: Condensation reaction <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
To determine the reaction type, we first need to identify the functional group present in the molecule. Looking at the structural formula, we see a carbonyl group ($C=O$) directly bonded to an oxygen atom that is part of a carbon chain. 

This specific arrangement, $-COO-$, identifies the compound as an **ester**. Identifying functional groups is a key skill covered in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**.

highlights.text: The compound below is commonly used in artificial fruit flavouring.

steps.title: Determine the reaction type
steps.content:
Esters are synthesized through the reaction of a carboxylic acid and an alcohol. During this process, the two molecules join together, and a small molecule (typically water, $H_2O$) is eliminated.

Any reaction where two molecules combine to form a larger molecule with the loss of a small molecule is classified as a **condensation reaction**. In this specific case, it is also known as esterification, but the broad category required by the IB syllabus for this process is condensation.

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content: First, let's look at the structure provided. The molecule contains the functional group $-COO-$, which identifies it as an **ester**. In IB Chemistry, you'll learn that esters are typically formed from the reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol.

steps.title: Identify the precursors
steps.content:
To understand how this molecule was formed, we can 'break' the ester bond (the $C-O$ single bond) to see the starting materials:

*   The part with the carbonyl group ($C=O$) comes from **ethanoic acid**: $CH_3COOH$
*   The part with the alkyl chain comes from **propan-1-ol**: $HOCH_2CH_2CH_3$

steps.title: Analyze the elimination
steps.content:
When these two precursors join together to form the ester linkage, a small molecule is 'squeezed out' or eliminated. 

Specifically, the $-OH$ group from the ethanoic acid and the $-H$ atom from the alcohol's hydroxyl group combine:

$$OH + H \rightarrow H_2O$$

This loss of a small molecule allows the remaining fragments to bond together to form the artificial flavoring.

steps.title: Classify the reaction
steps.content:
Because this reaction involves the joining of two molecules with the simultaneous **elimination of a small molecule** (water), it is classified as a **condensation reaction**.

In the context of polymers (which you can review in **Topic S2.4.6**), you will see this same logic applied to create long chains!

parts.content: Suggest why esters like ethyl butanoate are often used in perfumes and flavourings.
parts.markscheme: They are volatile and have pleasant fruity smells. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional group
steps.content:
The compound shown in the image is an **ester**. In organic chemistry, esters are represented by the general formula $R-COOR'$. This specific ester is ethyl butanoate, which is formed from the reaction between ethanol and butanoic acid.

This topic is covered in **S3.2 Functional groups**, where you learn to recognize different classes of organic compounds.

steps.title: Analyze sensory properties
steps.content:
A key physical characteristic of esters is their **aroma**. Unlike many organic compounds that have pungent or unpleasant odors, esters typically have **pleasant, fruity, or sweet smells**. 

For example, ethyl butanoate specifically provides a scent similar to pineapple, which is why it is used in artificial fruit flavorings.

steps.title: Evaluate volatility
steps.content:
To be used in perfumes or as a flavoring, a compound must be **volatile**. Volatility is the ability of a substance to evaporate easily into a gas. 

Because esters have relatively weak intermolecular forces (compared to carboxylic acids or alcohols of similar mass), they evaporate at low temperatures. This allows the molecules to enter the air and reach the olfactory receptors in our nose, making the scent detectable.

steps.title: Synthesize the final answer
steps.content: To earn the mark, you must mention both the physical state and the sensory effect. Esters are used because they are **volatile** (they vaporize easily) and have **pleasant/fruity smells**.

highlights.text: They are volatile and have pleasant fruity smells.

parts.content: Predict the products of the complete hydrolysis of ethyl butanoate under acidic conditions.
parts.markscheme:
Butanoic acid ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Ethanol ($\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand ester hydrolysis
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, it is essential to remember that hydrolysis is the chemical breakdown of a compound due to reaction with water. For an ester like **ethyl butanoate**, hydrolysis is the reverse of esterification. Under acidic conditions, the ester reacts with water to reform the original **carboxylic acid** and **alcohol** used to make it.

$$\text{Ester} + \text{Water} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{Carboxylic Acid} + \text{Alcohol}$$

highlights.text: complete hydrolysis of ethyl butanoate under acidic conditions

steps.title: Analyze the 'ethyl' prefix
steps.content:
Using **Nomenclature Analysis**, we look at the first part of the IUPAC name: **ethyl**. 

In ester naming, the first word always identifies the alkyl group that came from the **alcohol**. Since the prefix is 'ethyl' (representing a 2-carbon chain), the parent alcohol must be **ethanol**.

$$\text{Ethyl group} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \text{ (Ethanol)}$$

steps.title: Analyze the 'butanoate' suffix
steps.content:
Next, we look at the second part of the name: **butanoate**. 

The suffix **-oate** indicates the part of the molecule derived from the **carboxylic acid**. The root 'butan-' tells us there is a 4-carbon chain. Therefore, the parent carboxylic acid is **butanoic acid**.

$$\text{Butanoate group} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} \text{ (Butanoic acid)}$$

steps.title: Identify the final products
steps.content:
Under acidic conditions, these two components are released in their neutral forms. Unlike basic hydrolysis (saponification), which would produce a carboxylate salt, acidic hydrolysis yields the actual acid.

The two products are:
1.  **Butanoic acid** (1 mark)
2.  **Ethanol** (1 mark)

To review how these functional groups are named, you can check **Topic S3.2: Functional groups** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify the ester linkage
steps.content:
To predict the products of hydrolysis, we first identify the ester functional group in ethyl butanoate. An ester has the general structure $\text{R-COO-R'}$. In this specific molecule, the 'butanoate' part comes from a 4-carbon chain (including the carbonyl carbon), and the 'ethyl' part comes from a 2-carbon chain attached to the oxygen.

This is covered in **Structure 3.2: Functional groups**, where we learn to classify and name organic compounds.

highlights.text: complete hydrolysis of ethyl butanoate under acidic conditions

steps.title: Locate the cleavage site
steps.content:
Using the structural cleavage approach, we locate the single bond between the carbonyl carbon ($\text{C=O}$) and the alkoxy oxygen ($\text{-O-}$). During hydrolysis, this is the bond that breaks (cleaves).

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{C}(=\text{O}) \boldsymbol{-} \text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_3$$

steps.title: Add water components
steps.content:
Hydrolysis literally means "splitting with water." We add the components of a water molecule ($\text{H-OH}$) across the bond we just broke:

1. Add the hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) to the **carbonyl group** (the side that originally came from the acid).
2. Add the hydrogen atom ($-\text{H}$) to the **alkoxy group** (the side that originally came from the alcohol).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CO-} &+ \text{-OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} \\ \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{O-} &+ \text{-H} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Name the final products
steps.content:
The two products formed are:
- **Butanoic acid**: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$
- **Ethanol**: $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$

Note: Under acidic conditions, the carboxylic acid remains in its protonated form rather than forming a carboxylate salt.

parts.content: Describe how infrared (IR) spectroscopy could be used to distinguish ethyl butanoate from the carboxylic acid used to make it.
parts.markscheme:
IR spectrum of ethyl butanoate shows strong absorption around $1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ ($\mathrm{C=O}$ in ester). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Butanoic acid shows broad O–H stretch around $2500\text{--}3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the functional groups
steps.content:
To distinguish between these two compounds using IR spectroscopy, we first need to identify the functional groups present in each:

*   **Ethyl butanoate:** This is an ester. It contains a carbonyl group ($C=O$).
*   **Butanoic acid:** This is a carboxylic acid (the acid used to make the ester). It contains both a carbonyl group ($C=O$) and a hydroxyl group ($O-H$).

This question is related to **S3.2.9: Infrared spectra**, where we learn that different functional groups absorb specific frequencies of infrared radiation.

highlights.text: distinguish ethyl butanoate from the carboxylic acid

steps.title: Identify the O-H signal
steps.content:
The most significant difference is the presence of the hydroxyl group in the acid. 

Butanoic acid will show a very characteristic **broad** absorption band due to the $O-H$ stretch. According to the markscheme and standard IB data, this occurs in the range:

$$2500\text{--}3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

This broad peak will be absent in the IR spectrum of the ester (ethyl butanoate).

steps.title: Identify the C=O signal
steps.content:
Both molecules contain a carbonyl group, but they appear at slightly different wavenumbers. 

The IR spectrum of **ethyl butanoate** will show a strong, sharp absorption band for the $C=O$ bond at approximately:

$$1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

While the carboxylic acid also has a $C=O$ peak (usually around $1700\text{--}1725 \text{ cm}^{-1}$), the combination of the specific ester $C=O$ peak and the absence of the $O-H$ peak allows us to confirm the identity of the ester.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks, ensure you state both specific features:

1.  **Butanoic acid:** Look for the broad $O-H$ absorption at $2500\text{--}3300 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.
2.  **Ethyl butanoate:** Look for the strong $C=O$ absorption around $1750 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (and the absence of the broad $O-H$ peak).

steps.title: Identify the molecules
steps.content: To distinguish between the two substances, we first identify their functional groups. **Ethyl butanoate** is an ester, while **butanoic acid** is a carboxylic acid. IR spectroscopy identifies these based on how their bonds vibrate when absorbing infrared radiation.

steps.title: Analyze the Fingerprint Region
steps.content: One definitive way to identify a compound is by looking at the **fingerprint region**, which is the complex area of the spectrum typically below $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$. Each organic molecule has a unique, complex pattern of absorption peaks in this region, much like a human fingerprint.

steps.title: Compare with Reference Standards
steps.content: To distinguish the ester from the acid using this method, the IR spectrum of the unknown sample is compared against a **database of known reference standards**. If the peaks below $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$ match the reference spectrum for ethyl butanoate exactly, the identity is confirmed.

steps.title: Identify Diagnostic Peaks
steps.content:
While the fingerprint region is unique, IB markschemes usually require you to identify the major functional group peaks in the diagnostic region (above $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$):

1.  **Ethyl butanoate (Ester):** Shows a strong, sharp absorption for the $\text{C=O}$ bond around $1750\text{ cm}^{-1}$.
2.  **Butanoic acid (Carboxylic Acid):** Shows a very broad $\text{O-H}$ absorption stretch between $2500$ and $3300\text{ cm}^{-1}$ (often overlapping with $\text{C-H}$ peaks).

ti84.instructions: Add the upper and lower limits of the range and divide by 2.

desmos.instructions: Find the midpoint of the O-H stretch range.

calculator.description: Calculate the center of the broad O-H absorption range for carboxylic acids.

steps.title: Summarize for the Markscheme
steps.content:
To secure both marks, state that:
- The ester spectrum has a strong $\text{C=O}$ peak at $\approx 1750\text{ cm}^{-1}$.
- The acid spectrum has a broad $\text{O-H}$ peak at $\approx 2500\text{--}3300\text{ cm}^{-1}$.

You can further note that these compounds are definitively distinguished by comparing their unique patterns in the **fingerprint region** ($< 1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$) to reference spectra.

steps.content: To form dichloromethane ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$), the reaction proceeds through a series of radical propagation steps. Since each step replaces one hydrogen atom with one chlorine atom, we must go through two distinct stages of substitution: first forming chloromethane, and then reacting that product further.

highlights.text: propagation steps involved in the formation of dichloromethane

steps.title: Stage 1: Methane to Chloromethane
steps.content:
In the first stage, a chlorine radical ($\text{Cl}\bullet$) abstracts a hydrogen atom from methane to create a methyl radical. This methyl radical then reacts with a chlorine molecule ($\text{Cl}_2$) to form chloromethane and regenerate the chlorine radical.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Cl}\bullet + \text{CH}_4 &\to \bullet\text{CH}_3 + \text{HCl} \\ \bullet\text{CH}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 &\to \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Stage 2: Chloromethane to Dichloromethane
steps.content:
Because chlorine is in excess, the chloromethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$) produced in the first stage undergoes the exact same two-step process to reach dichloromethane ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Cl}\bullet + \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} &\to \bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HCl} \\ \bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}_2 &\to \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Cl}\bullet \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Review marks and formatting
steps.content: According to the markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the first pair of equations (Stage 1) and 1 mark for the second pair (Stage 2). Ensure your radical dots ($\bullet$) are clearly visible. While IB allows the dot on either the carbon or chlorine, placing it on the carbon (e.g., $\bullet\text{CH}_3$) is the preferred chemical representation to show where the unpaired electron actually resides.

parts.content: Write a termination step that would prevent further formation of $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ from $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$.
parts.markscheme:
$2\text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{Cl}_2$
<marks>A1</marks>

**OR**

$\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$
<marks>A1</marks>

**OR**

$2\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \to \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{Cl}_2$
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the mechanism stage
steps.content: The chlorination of methane is a **radical substitution** reaction involving three stages: initiation, propagation, and termination. To stop the reaction and prevent the further formation of products, we need a **termination step**. Termination occurs when two radicals collide and react to form a stable molecule, effectively removing the reactive species from the system.

steps.title: Apply Halogen Radical Recombination
steps.content:
In the **Halogen Radical Recombination** approach, we focus on the radicals formed during the initiation or propagation steps. Specifically, we show two chlorine radicals ($\text{Cl}\bullet$) reacting together. 

When two chlorine radicals collide, they pair their unpaired electrons to form a covalent bond, resulting in a stable chlorine molecule ($\text{Cl}_2$).

steps.title: Write the termination equation
steps.content:
By removing the chlorine radicals, they are no longer available to react with $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ in the propagation stage. This prevents the sequence that leads to dichloromethane.

The chemical equation for this termination step is:

$$2\text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{Cl}_2$$

highlights.text: prevent further formation of CH2Cl2 from CH3Cl

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The reaction $2\text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{Cl}_2$ is a valid termination step because it reduces the concentration of radicals needed to continue the chain reaction. 

According to the markscheme, showing this recombination earns **1 mark** (A1).

steps.title: Understand termination steps
steps.content:
In a radical substitution mechanism, a **termination step** occurs when two radicals collide and react to form a stable molecule. Because this removes the highly reactive radicals from the system without producing new ones, it stops (terminates) the chain reaction. 

This question asks for a specific termination step that prevents the conversion of chloromethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$) into dichloromethane ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$). To do this, we need to remove the radicals that lead to that product.

highlights.text: prevent further formation of CH_2Cl_2 from CH_3Cl

steps.title: Identify the chloromethyl radical
steps.content:
To form dichloromethane ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$) from chloromethane ($\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$), the propagation steps first create a **chloromethyl radical** ($\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$):

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet \rightarrow \bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$$

If this radical reacts further with $\text{Cl}_2$, it would produce dichloromethane. Therefore, any termination step that "uses up" this $\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$ radical will prevent it from becoming $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$.

steps.title: Write the dimerization equation
steps.content:
Using the **Haloalkane Radical Dimerization** approach, we show two of these chloromethyl radicals colliding. This forms a larger halogenated alkane called 1,2-dichloroethane.

The balanced equation for this termination step is:

$$2\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{Cl}_2$$

This removes the $\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$ radicals from the reaction mixture, successfully preventing the further formation of $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$.

steps.title: Understand radical termination
steps.content: In a radical substitution mechanism, **termination** occurs when two free radicals collide and react to form a stable molecule. Because this process consumes radicals without producing new ones, it stops the chain reaction. This topic is covered in **Reactivity 3.3: Electron sharing reactions**.

steps.title: Identify the specific radicals
steps.content:
To specifically form dichloromethane ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$) through **Mixed Radical Recombination**, we must identify the two different radicals that contain the necessary atoms:

1.  The **chloromethyl radical**: $\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$ (produced during the propagation of methyl chloride)
2.  The **chlorine radical**: $\text{Cl}\bullet$ (available from the initiation step or propagation steps)

highlights.text: chloromethyl radical and a chlorine radical combine to form dichloromethane

steps.title: Write the recombination equation
steps.content:
When these two radicals meet, their unpaired electrons pair up to form a new covalent bond. The resulting equation represents a termination step because no radicals remain on the product side:

$$\bullet\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$$

According to the markscheme, this specific recombination earns the mark as it directly prevents further propagation from $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ by locking the species into the stable dichloromethane form.
